note_data.title: Hydrocarbons
keyPoints: **Alkanes** are saturated hydrocarbons with the general formula CnH2n+2
**Alkenes** are unsaturated hydrocarbons with the general formula CnH2n containing a C=C double bond
Alkanes undergo **substitution reactions** where one atom/group is replaced by another atom/group
Alkenes undergo **addition reactions** where atoms/groups are added across the C=C double bond
**Cracking** is the thermal decomposition of long-chain alkanes to produce shorter alkanes and alkenes
Alkenes can undergo **addition polymerisation** to form long-chain polymers
Alkanes show **free radical substitution** with chlorine in the presence of UV light
**Bromine water** decolourises from orange to colourless when alkenes are present - this is a test for unsaturation
Cracking requires high temperature (450-750°C) and a catalyst (zeolite or aluminium oxide)

definitions.definition: A hydrocarbon containing only single C-C bonds
A hydrocarbon containing at least one C=C double bond
A reaction where atoms or groups are added to a molecule without anything being removed
A reaction where one atom or group is replaced by another atom or group
The joining together of many unsaturated monomers to form a long-chain polymer

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give conditions and observations. Use 'when...then' format.

modelAnswer.question: Explain why alkenes are more reactive than alkanes. [3]
modelAnswer.answer: Alkenes contain a C=C double bond which makes them unsaturated. The C=C double bond has a high electron density which attracts electrophiles. Alkenes can undergo addition reactions whereas alkanes are saturated and can only undergo substitution reactions under harsh conditions.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Alkenes contain C=C double bond/are unsaturated
Mark 2: High electron density attracts electrophiles
Mark 3: Alkenes undergo addition reactions/alkanes need harsh conditions

commonMistakes: Never say 'alkenes are unstable' - always say 'alkenes are reactive due to the C=C double bond'
Students often forget to mention UV light is needed for free radical substitution
Don't confuse addition and substitution - addition adds atoms, substitution replaces atoms
Never say cracking 'breaks' bonds - say 'thermal decomposition occurs'
Students often draw incorrect polymer structures - ensure the C=C bond opens completely
examinerTips: Hydrocarbon reactions appear in every Paper 2 - learn mechanisms thoroughly
Always state conditions clearly: temperature, catalyst, and UV light where needed
For polymer questions, show at least 3 repeat units with continuation bonds
Bromine water test is frequently tested - remember orange to colourless colour change
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Free radical substitution mechanism showing initiation (Cl2 → 2Cl•), propagation (CH4 + Cl• → CH3• + HCl, then CH3• + Cl2 → CH3Cl + Cl•) and termination steps
